JMG,

1. I’ve been doing the Middle Pillar consistently every day for about a year, and after each repetition I feel like I just completed a moderately intense workout in terms of the level of exertion involved. I have sweat running down my forehead and feel like I need to lie down for a couple of minutes. Is my etheric body getting activated? Does something similar happen at all levels of being, not just etheric (astral, mental, etc.)?

2. Is discursive meditation valuable even for “cerebral” types who are in their own heads a lot? If you tend to be in a semi-meditative state most of the time, thinking about a topic and drawing associations, do you still stand to gain as much benefit from the practice as someone who is more “in the body”?

3. A common element of occult philosophy is the notion of spiritual improvement through a succession of lifetimes until the soul moves past the human level. However, certain occultists have also talked about humanity as already being a perfect reflection of the macrocosm, since we have all the levels of being represented in us (spiritual, mental, astral, etheric, and physical bodies). In that sense, we are more ‘perfect’ than the angels and gods, who lack physical bodies. Dion Fortune once made a comment along the lines that humanity stands in relation to the angels as a three-year old child stands in relation to a three-year old dog. Is it really an improvement for us to shed the physical body in the next stage of our development? Wouldn't that be a loss of an essential part of ourselves?

4. I’ve been reading through Damien Echols’ book High Magick and one of the thaumaturgical practices he describes is called “Dragon Cultivates the Golden Pearl” and consists of creating a thoughtform, imbuing it with an intention, and releasing it into the universe. I’m curious how this practice relates to evocation of spirits in the GD tradition. Is there a need for evocatory protections (magic circle, asafetida, etc.), and should one refrain from banishing afterwards in order not to cancel out the ritual? Is there a limit to the number of “Golden Pearls” one should have running concurrently?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sure. Let's start with a larger image:
[IMAGE:0]

The instrument is called a monochord -- it's a single string with a flat fretboard, the ancestor of the mountain dulcimer. It used to be the standard way to teach music theory -- you hand your student a monochord, have them lay out various geometrical progressions on paper, slip the paper over the fretboard and finger the string at each point along the progressions, listening to the notes and thus learning the unity of geometry and music.

Fludd here is using the monochord to explain the planes of being as understood in Renaissance magic. The lowest notes -- Γ, A, B, and C -- are the four material elements, Earth, Water, Air, and Fire. The next octave, D through c, is the seven planetary spheres; d is the sphere of fixed stars, and the three notes above that, e, f, and gg, are the three levels of the angelic world. Yes, that's God's hand adjusting the tuning up top. The various arcs on the two sides indicate mathematical and harmonic proportions between the planes.

replies.title: Antennas & Monochords
replies.content:
For those who may be interested, here is a short piece I wrote on the relationship between antennas and monochords:

http://www.sothismedias.com/home/tuning-the-terrestrial-monochord

(The key word in thinking about this relationship should be "harmonics").

The folkie congnoscenti among readers here will surely recognize this image from the Anthology of American Folk Music compiled by magician Harry Everett Smith, whose "working" in putting the compilation together, really did kickstart the folk music revival.

The original set was divided into "Ballads" "Social music" and "Songs" and were alchemically color coded green, red and blue for water, fire and air. A fourth "Earth" volume was planned, but arguments with the producers of the set got in the way of it coming out until much later. Supposedly the argument was about them wanting to include a song by the Delmore Brothers about the re-election of FDR, and he didn't like it so stopped it from being released.

Harry Smith was more than a colorful individual. It's too bad he was such a severe alcoholic, as I can imagine how much more he could have accomplished if he hadn't been suffering from that disease/allergy/spiritual sickness.

replies.title: Re: Antennas & Monochords
replies.content: Thanks for the link. As for Harry Smith, don't be too sure of that. It's an unwelcome discovery often repeated among some creative alcoholics that if they sober up, their creativity goes away.

replies.title: Re: Antennas & Monochords
replies.content:
I then suppose for those problem drinkers who have that experience it must be a difficult choice, between a creative life or a sober life.

I didn't mean to cast judgment on Smith, only noting from having read the one book about him I'm aware of (American Magus)(and still hoping for the biography rumored to being written by John Szwa)the various problems it caused him.

On the other hand are the alcoholic creatives who their capacity to get to work increased when they aren't mired with hangovers, chasing drinks & thrills, and interpersonal drama that often comes with it. I'm sure there is lots of variation between the two poles.

The choice is of course up to each individual.

You're take on it reminded though of a story from David Lynch. He was going to see a therapist for some of his issues and asked the therapist if it would effect his creativity. The therapist said it might, so he didn't get the therapy.

All this makes me think of artists and "sensitives" who often use alcohol or other drugs as a way to desensitize themselves to all the "vibes" and impressions they pick up. It would be nice if society recognized this to a greater degree, and various tools for coping, outside of getting plastered, could b e developed. I've found some that work for me. It may be something to write about later.

& that said I know plenty of people who don't have problems with alcohol, etc. What people put into their bodies is their own business.

Thanks, & happy magic monday.

replies.title: Re: Antennas & Monochords
replies.content:
Stephen King has said that’s why he put off sobering up for as long as possible, he feared doing so would impair his creativity. Tragically, he was right. His sober stuff is not nearly as good as the stuff he wrote as an alcoholic.

—Lady Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: King, Booze, Hemingway, Coltrane, Opioids
replies.content:
Maybe some of his best works were written while he was an alcoholic, but he has still written some really good novels in his Later years.

(The brand new one, Later, which I just read, is a nice crime-ghost story. I like all the stuff he's been putting out via Hardcase Crime -an excellent publishing imprint in general.)

I don't read all his new stuff by any means though. I think most of the Dark Tower series was finished when he'd gotten sober and that is some of his best work.

I don't really buy into the "dark and tormented -and drug addled- artist" trope. Sure, there are plenty of them, but it doesn't always end well. One of the ways that can end is the way it ended for Ernest Hemingway.

All the booze in the world didn't make Hemingway any good as a writer in the first place. So booze can obviously also make your writing/art really bad.

Yet, perhaps when some people who've been drinking and drugging quit they turn to other things. Arthur Rimbaud left us the dazzling "Season in Hell" as he went through furious withdraws. After that he lived a life as a gun runner.

And there are people like John Coltrane, amazing musicians, while he was suffering alcoholism and heroin addiction, and still making the most beautiful records afterwards.

When I see all the people I know and the ones on the streets I don't know whose lives have been ripped apart by alcohol, opiates, meth and the like, when they have so much potential, I wish for them and the loved ones in their lives better things.

Yet in general I'll do my best to suspend judgment about substance use & abuse, in the arts or otherwise.

comments.title: Cosmic Doctrine
comments.content:
Hello JMG. When you began your bookclub studying the Cosmic Doctrine in June, 2018, I was all ready to jump on-board and get down and dirty with it. But after 3 months, I felt like I was spinning my wheels, and felt too discouraged to continue.

Each month, a new installment would roll by which I would never read, and I would feel rueful. I decided on this April new moon to take it up again.

I assume you recall my flurry of comments over the last month or so relating the ongoing, baleful influence of my dysfunctional upbringing, and my ongoing and new efforts to cope with it, i.e. the deep, inaccessible mental processes that project a shameful self-image, and an invisible “inner saboteur” who tricks me into dysfunctional speech and behavior, resulting in my having few relationships, and the most important ones damaged. I have been able to interpret some key dreams (spanning decades) which I now think give me some traction with this problem.

This has motivated me to re-approach The Cosmic Doctrine. I’m on Chapter 1, and an application to my situation has occurred to me. I seek your guidance as to whether I’m on the right track, or just lost in the weeds:

In this application, I would liken the Ring-cosmos to the pattern of behavior my parents developed after I was born, along with my reactions to them. The Ring Chaos would map to the self-image/self-concept that develops in the child as an outcome of the relationship. The pivoting and arcing, attraction/repulsion between these rings would map to the mutual feedback between parents and child. So the harmful self-image certainly fits as being both Prime Evil and Primeval; and it is also by definition here the “Thrust Block”, the thing I resist on push off from in order to go somewhere else or do something else.

Is my application valid so far? Do you see potential for it being helpful?

—Lunar Apprentice

replies.title: Re: Cosmic Doctrine
replies.content: You're mostly there, but I'd reverse the symbolism. The Ring-Cosmos of any system is its own internal structure; its Ring-Chaos is provided by its environment. So your self-image and self-concept is your Ring-Cosmos, and the behavior of your parents in response to you defined your original Ring-Chaos. Your Ring-Pass-Not, in turn, is your sense of boundaries, which may not yet be well formed -- but it's the pivoting rotation of the Ring-Cosmos relative to the Ring-Chaos that eventually forms it.

replies.title: Re: Cosmic Doctrine
replies.content:
Exactly what John Michael responded. You've placed your parents at the center of your own cosmos, leaving very little space for you to unfold your Self in.

Your universe rotates in the direction that your soul's will/intention/purpose incarnated this go round in order to explore. Your parents' universes rotated in whatever way they may have needed, thereby providing your soul with some of the challenges it was looking for.

Your parents/teachers/culture establish a Ring-Chaos to keep you from enthusiastically flying out with abandon into the chaos. That restricting, constraining influence is always traumatic, but equally necessary. Something has to keep each of us centered in the rotation we actually came here to experience.

My best guess is that the harmful self-image is a particularly constricting Ring-Chaos that keeps you trapped in a limited section of your much larger cosmos. Using it as a thrust block to go somewhere else and explore your full Self sounds like a very powerful technique. Your actual Ring-Chaos is probably much vaster than what your parents were ever comfortable with.

As traumatizing as our parents, pathologies, and environment can be, they are precisely what we came here to experience. Why on earth I would have chosen my particular life, with all its challenges, is beyond my capacity to fathom, but choose it I did. If we never closed off parts of ourselves, we would never get to experience the wonder of discovering that there is so much more to us than we had dared imagine.

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: Cosmic Doctrine
replies.content:
I don't have a spiritual perspective here, but what you mention does bring to mind an easy to read pop-psych book that might be useful to you: "Games people play" by Eric Berne, an introduction to Transactional Analysis.

But with the punch line, you might not even need to read the whole joke. Concept is that within each of us are states of mental being in which we behave like children who look to an external source of guidance, or like parents who are imposing that guidance on children, or like adults behaving with equal mutual respect towards our fellow adults. The child state can be one of fun, joy, fresh discovery, and can also be one of acting-out. The parental state can be one of guidance, nurture, wise direction, or of harsh control efforts. Much of our conversation and interaction with other people as in implicit game such as "I'm going to act up until you show me you care, Mummy and Daddy, by giving me some attention," or, "I'm the adult in charge, you unruly kids pipe down!" when those assumed roles are unhealthy to live out in the present moment.

Might be some food for meditation and journaling here.

I continue to wish you the best of easy flow towards a state of inner peace and complete personal integrity, free to participate in the present moment with delight and power, unburdened by hardships imposed in the past.

- Mr. New-Writer

replies.title: Re: Cosmic Doctrine
replies.content:
Eric Berne is a great read. I got much from him. I think that Claude Steiner's elaboration in Scripts People Live is even better, as it just collapses all the games into just a few categories.

--Lunar Apprentice

comments.title: Follow-up
comments.content:
JMG, as regards your advice two weeks on cultivating the reliving of a key memory of my Aunt’s love, I have been following your advice assiduously. While I have yet to succeed in reliving the memory, it certainly is a pleasant mediation, and I have no difficulty motivating myself to do it. I have been able to connect some other, more diffuse recollections of my experiences with my Aunt with this dream (referenced in my comment 2 weeks ago), and I’ve also come to recognize some odd associations of positive emotions (a feeling of being loved) with certain places, objects, smells, sounds, environments and such; all of these associations arise from childhood experiences with my Aunt.

Already, I’ve realized benefits. I no longer get angry when my daughters act out, even in outrageous ways. I not only don’t act angry, I don’t feel angry. Our interactions have become less tense. Just yesterday, my younger daughter (10) asked me to take just her on a hike in the mountains, a first! My daughters and I have had several meals out, which is not new, but my older daughter actually conversed with me rather than sitting absorbed in her smart-phone, and both of them giving me advice on how to be more “with it”. My former wife has noticed the improvement, and said that whatever I’m doing now, she wished I were doing it years ago. Even her dog gets more exited when I come by.

I’m also following your suggestion a month or so back to read Dion Fortune’s novels. I’ve finished The Secrets of Dr. Taverner, and of course The Goat Foot God, and am well into The Winged Bull. For me, these novels feel significant, pertinent, and appropriate. Several of the main characters seem much like me. (I started The Demon Lover, but found the main characters, Veronica and Lucas, so lacking in relatability or even believability, that I set it aside.) I can’t say I’ve processed this reading, or had any result, but again, it feels valuable. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Congratulations! At some point you managed to awaken to one of the parts of your deeper self — specifically, the part that is best at assessing the dimension of fate. I know that sounds strange, but fate is a measurable dimension, although the one we are absolutely the least adept at assessing. Given your oddly sensory experience of it's variations, I would guess that your awakening occurred in your early childhood. When did you first notice your sensory premonitions?

Mostly, when adults awaken to a part of the deeper self, their ability to experience its dimension as a felt sense has long since been lost. Young children still have those senses, so awakening at a particularly young age can keep that sense accessible through a full lifetime.

The exorcist Rachel Stavis is an example of someone who awakened while very young. Unlike you, she awakened to the part that is best at assessing the dimension of charge, so she can still see demons (And now you know why babies won't stop crying in certain people's arms — they can see something there that we can't!) Like you, she was none too happy with that talent initially.

Unfortunately, I don't know of any way you can undo your awakening — you have an increased ability to sense fate. Whether you want to relate to that as a nuisance or a gift is completely up to you, but "a pointless exercise of awareness" it most certainly is not. Learning to skillfully navigate it and discovering how you might be able to collaborate with that part of your deeper self sounds to me like a very interesting adventure.

If you're the mother, it's not impossible that you somehow passed on your ability to your children while they were in utero. However, I think it's more likely that as a parent you just never encouraged your babies to suppress their ability to sense fate. As they grew up and noticed that their parent had that sense in abundance, keeping it as open as possible seemed perfectly normal to your kids.

— Christophe

I know a fair number of people who read Tarot and haven't heard of this practice. However, some readers never allow anyone else to handle their deck, while others, when doing a reading for someone else, hand the deck to the querent to shuffle. It's also common to have a special box or bag to hold the cards when they aren't being used.

In my opinion and experience, the cards themselves are not particularly susceptible to absorbing influences just by being laid out in a pattern for people to look at. So if you don't allow other people to handle your deck, and you put them away or leave them on a protected altar when not in active use, no cleansing is necessary.

replies.title: Re: Cleasning Tarot Cards
replies.content:
I have some familiarity with the course you mentioned and with the occultist you hinted at.

I don't think this has anything to do with doing 'negative' magic, but with etheric residue that the cards can pick up when doing readings about things that have heavy emotional charges, or are on subjects such as when you are doing a reading about curses being placed on a querent or when negative magic is being used against you. Or if you are dealing with nasty beings in a capacity as an exorcist, etc. It's not that you are doing 'negative' magic, but that etheric crud can get on the cards. It could also be if you are doing readings about death, etc., for someone who is working in a role of psychopomp, etc.

Think of giving your cards a salt shaking in the same way some people here on this forum take a hoodoo salt water bath. The salt strips some of the etheric goop off of the cards.

The occultist you mentioned also has a new book out on tarot. It is quite awesome. Her layouts, many not seen in any other books I know about, and her instructions for how to frame questions when using the tarot are well worth it, as are her series of esoteric explications on the inner aspects of the trumps.

I have found her to be a trustworthy and innovative & yet traditional magical teacher in the western mystery tradition. Some of the threads woven in to her work have been W.E. Butler, Dion Fortune's magical order (it's descendants), R.J. Stewart's work (to whom she was married for a time and worked with him as an adept), William Gray, among others. The inner plane contacts are the real light that shines through her books and the course she made. It's all very solid stuff.

Of course your mileage may vary.

The more magical schools out there for students to pick from the better, IMO. Then there is a greater chance for the apprentice to find the right fit for them. And then to cross-pollinate with each other as is often the case.

replies.title: Re: Nine questions
replies.content:
8) Midnight is the conventional witching hour, but that goes back to preindustrial times when candles were expensive, the hearth fire was banked before people went to bed, and there was no TV. Most people went to bed a few hours after sunset unless they had a specific reason to stay up, like watching over a sick child or doing agricultural tasks that couldn't be put off. Once ordinary folk were asleep in their beds, the witches came out.

In industrial cities, people stay up later because they can or because they must. I've done shift work and I've also when younger done some after hours clubbing. In the absence of stimulants apart from light, noise, and caffeine, most people's circadian rhythms drop to a low point around 3 AM. Even the entertainment district does not have much activity between 3 AM and 5 AM. Most people are not only in bed but sound asleep. If you have a magical working to do that benefits from a quiet atmosphere in both the physical realm and the psychic realm, the hour from 3 AM to 4 AM usually has the best conditions.

Lady Cutekitten, my thoughts on my own self-publishing plans have developed a bit. Perhaps something here might help you.

Do you literally mean 1,200 pages? I think that's big enough that it would make more sense to break it into multiple volumes, sold separately.

Even if I really loved all 1,200 pages of your story, I'd much rather get it as a series of books that don't double as gym workout supplies.

Buying a book (or book subscription) based only on the first line? I think that's a rather big leap to ask. I'd be reluctant to consider it even from a favorite author whose work I already loved.

I want readers to have ample opportunity to get a feel for the characters, setting, plot, writing style, and themes, before I ask them for money.

It's fun for me to watch "restaurant turnaround" shows. Seems that they always find some way to offer some free meals to people in the community.

Imagine a new restaurant where they come by your house, put one drop of their wonderful mustard on your tongue - and that's all you get to know about their cuisine. Then they immediately hit you up for a $50 dinner reservation. Is that really enough of a sample to know what you're getting?

Compare that to the restaurant that gives you a little sampler plate, a few bites each of a salad, a main dish, a dessert. Now you know what they're about.

Or: Thanks for meeting me for coffee, here on our first date. Nice to meet you. Would you like cream and sugar? And while I get that, by the way which side of my bed will you prefer for our honeymoon next week? Just maybe it's moving a bit too fast...

I plan to make the first several chapters available for free. My giant SAY WHAT? plot twist is around 100 pages in, and I'm a bit worried about losing people there! I can't imagine feeling good about a writer who slams in a Game Of Thrones level of letdown-twist at the end.

I expect to sign up for both Patreon and Subscribe Star. Both are free of sign-up or monthly fees. It seems to me that most of the Patreon kick-outs had to do with currently controversial political themes, primarily red state/right side of the aisle, or else with pornography.

My first novel and add-on bonuses don't take any hard-nosed political stands. They also have no explicit sex. I think I'm likely well within the limits of Patreon's newer, stricter policies. They do post their policies. You can read and decide for yourself if you feel you'd be too close to their lines.

Some other thoughts about pricing, bonuses, and promotions are starting to come together for me. But I don't want to take up too much of JMG's open house for magic to discuss them.

Hope this helps as you clarify your own plans.

— Mr. New-Writer

comments.title: question
comments.content:
Greetings JMG,

I'm curious about how karma actually works. Like are things such as degree of harm, or intention of harm, factoring into the karmic equation, and similarly, how does doing good work?

replies.title: Re: question
replies.content:
That's a very complicated question. The basic rule is that every thought, word, and deed has consequences for good or ill, and those return to you sooner or later. How they return, and how soon they return, depend on what other karmic patterns you're dealing with -- think of yourself as being in a field of constantly changing karmic forces that are all interacting with one another, and with you.

Yes, your attitude and intention matters, but that doesn't mean you get a free pass by saying "But I didn't mean it!" If you caused harm via very common combination of good intentions and stupidity, say, you get the karma of harm, the karma of having ineffective good intentions, and the karma of being an idiot. On the flip side, if you did something for selfish reasons that turned out to help someone, you get the karma of helping someone and also the karma of being selfish, and those tangle with each other and work out in due time.

replies.title: Re: question
replies.content:
I can see how the power of forgiveness might work. If someone has harmed me immensely, but I forgive them unconditionally for the act (or vice versa), then the karma could be lessened? I think the person being forgiven really has to be contrite, though.

Jon
tangential tangerine cactus

replies.title: Re: question
replies.content: That can have some effect, but as you say, it's a lessening rather than an erasure.

replies.title: Re: question
replies.content:
Imagine that a driver drove off the highway, and struck a pedestrian on the side of the road.

The pedestrian has the option to say, "I understand you made a mistake, and I release you from any obligation to pay my medical bills."

Whether or not the pedestrian does so, the driver is still off the road, with a smashed up car.

This is how I currently see forgiveness. I can choose to give the gift of relinquishing my own claims. Nobody else can impose on me an obligation to do so. This is forgiveness, if I choose it.

Meanwhile, the entire rest of the karma is still in play. The driver's not going to get very far on foot. They have some other imbalances they need to resolve, before they can make any more progress on their journey.

- Mr. New-Writer

replies.title: Re: question
replies.content:

I'm not really sure who suffered more, me or all the involuntary witnesses to my clueless duncedom. Is it possible I've already worked off my karma just by having to endure myself for so long?

— Christophe

replies.title: Re: question
replies.content: There are three ways to work off karma. There's the path of suffering -- you get slapped in the face by the consequences of your actions until you get a clue. There's the path of labor -- you get a clue and work hard at undoing the problems you caused. There's the path of insight -- you realize right down to the core just how stupid you were being, and the experience catalyzes change in your consciousness and thus in your actions. Most of the time, we get a mix of all three -- we get slapped in the face, get a clue, start working on changing things, and gradually figure things out. The fact that you recognize the problem is a good start. How much has your behavior changed?

comments.title: Dark times
comments.content: What role do you think the occult sciences could play helping us through the dark times ahead?  Not only in keeping ourselves centered, but  physically healing the environment

replies.title: Re: Dark times
replies.content:
Occultism by itself is not an effective way of causing major changes on the material plane. You can use occult means as a way to strengthen actions taken on the material plane, but if you're not doing the material actions, the occult actions won't amount to much. (I routinely field questions from people who want to use magic to heal the environment while continuing to wallow in lifestyles that wreck the environment, so if I seem a little irritable about this, there's a reason.)

If more people were to take up occult studies and practices, the resulting self-knowledge and insight would make it much easier for them to recognize that lives of endless material consumption aren't making them happy or fulfilled, and that can help them ditch destructive lifestyles and also cope with the hard limits of the deindustrial future. If more people who are already working to heal the environment were to combine occultism with their ecological restoration, that would make the restoration more effective. So there are possibilities -- but it's still going to take a lot of hard physical labor and a lot of giving up of ecologically damaging lifestyles.

comments.title: To the Future - And Beyond!
comments.content:
Here's one that's been bugging me for a while...is the concept of reincarnation part of the Myth of Progress? After all, despite the occasional setback, the journey of each soul is from "least evolved" (e.g. flatworm) to "evolved beyond our understanding" (astral plane...and beyond?). So the concept of evolution here seems to be "higher/lower" rather than "becoming better adapted to its environment." It seems to me that at least superficially this is no different than "progress forever."

Cordially,

RPC

replies.title: Re: To the Future - And Beyond!
replies.content: We're becoming better adapted to an environment that includes spiritual as well as material factors. Progress covertly supposes that everything is supposed to keep on improving in the same way forever -- bigger and better machines, or what have you -- but the basic idea of spiritual evolution as I understand it is that each soul develops in a different way, and so your soul could evolve in a completely different direction than mine.

replies.title: Re: To the Future - And Beyond!
replies.content:
Reincarnation isn't about higher/lower; it's about balance. Getting the Self into balance is an exceptionally complex endeavor. Getting the higher self into balance with the other higher selves it is a part of takes many, many lifetimes. The game being played here in the material world is just a small part of the game being played the next level up.

What looks to us like going from least evolved to evolved way beyond us is just the process of slowly adding more complexity with each incarnation. What's evolving is the ability to balance. It's not progress; it's learning a new skill. The higher selves are learning to balance in ever more complex configurations. When the material incarnation's complexity eventually matches the higher self's complexity, achieving balance in the lower self naturally achieves balance in the higher self. That's the last go round — you win!

— Christophe

comments.title: Occult History
comments.content:
Hello Mr Greer,

As you know, there is a long line of occult thinkers who claim to have access to secret knowledge from a bygone era but instead invented new ideas and rituals. I was wondering if you believed the legends about some kind of ancient Babylon/anti antediluvian/Atlantis/fill in the blank ancient magical empire, or is most of that just allegory? If so, how successful do you think we have been in recreating what was lost?

replies.title: Re: Occult History
replies.content: There's a lot of allegory, no question, but it's clear (for example) that the ancient Egyptians knew much more about magic than we do. Some of that lore was systematized in Roman times, and then mostly lost with the coming of Christianity; the Renaissance saw a lot of good work done to recover the late classical synthesis, and then much of that was lost with the coming of the scientific revolution. What we have now are fragments of a much vaster and more effective knowledge, though some of the fragments work well and more bits are being added all the time.

comments.title: Karma and reincarnation
comments.content:
Last week you speculated that particularly evil people, e.g. Hitler, would have a rotten afterlife in the lower astral plane for a while and then reincarnate in some life where they'd be brutally abused and murdered themselves, probably repeated several times. While the former part seems like natural consequences (if you are a real lowlife, your soul won't be fit to enter higher planes or subplanes), how do you envision the latter working? If, as many assume, people choose the lives that they enter into, I'd think even the worst of people would not voluntarily embark upon a life that they could see would contain nothing but child abuse and neglect, unless someone or something either drove or directly forced them to it. Or is the lower astral so rotten a place that souls will flee it whenever they can, even into a bad life? (And if so, who or what keeps them from grabbing better lives? Some apparently innately evil people are born to healthy and comfortable lives, e.g. Ted Bundy.)

As a side issue, I have the same question about kids who are born with cancer, say, and quickly die of it - who would want those lives? What soul would agree to enter a baby that would die miserably before it was old enough to live? I would hate to presume that suffering children were being punished for sins in a past life. But I also doubt whether it would be possible really to know in advance, even from a higher-plane perspective, what would happen in a life, because so many events are stochastic or dependent on others' choices not yet made. If you've pondered these issues, would you mind sharing a few of your thoughts on them? Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Karma and reincarnation
replies.content: Thank you for writing this question. I had very similar thoughts from last weeks Magic Monday

replies.title: Re: Karma and reincarnation
replies.content:
This notion that you get to choose your incarnations is a typical example of the modern cult of entitlement. No, you don't get to choose your next life. If you've reached a fairly high level of spiritual attainment, you can have some influence over it, but before then it's as reflexive as the birth process. So the soul that had been Hitler's, let's say, landed on the lowest level of the astral plane and was stuck there for a good long while as it processed the experiences of its Hitler-life, and then the earth-pull took effect and the soul was dragged back down into incarnation. Rinse and repeat, until eventually enough of that soul's karma was burnt off and it could begin to do something else with its lives.

As far as knowing in advance the shape of a life, remember that the higher you go on the planes, the les rigid time is. The highest planes are eternal, which means outside of time; a being on those planes can see all of time at a glance, and know what (from our perspective) will happen to you tomorrow, because from that being's perspective tomorrow has already happened and so has the end of life on earth two billion years from now.

replies.title: Re: Karma and reincarnation
replies.content: The fascinating part of this is that I thought that Hitler like behaviour would result in the unknown death - but maybe that has to take many, many lives of Hitler like behaviour?

replies.title: Re: Karma and reincarnation
replies.content:
The Unknown Death isn't just a matter of sheer nastiness. It happens when a soul that has awakened far enough to become aware of its own higher self and its destiny rejects those and clings to its ego instead. Since the higher self is the real identity of which the individual ego is a temporary expression, that causes a rupture between the lower and higher selves, and the lower self and everything that has gone into making it -- all the experiences of all the lives leading up to that moment -- is cut off from its own source, and dies utterly and forever when the body dies.

To judge by his biography, Hitler was nowhere near that point. He's got a long bitter road ahead of him, but not that. The Unknown Death does not happen very often at all.

replies.title: Re: Karma and reincarnation
replies.content:
Hmm, that second paragraph raises a bunch of interesting questions!

How does that mesh with free will? I'm suddenly thinking of that scene at the end of C. S. Lewis' The Great Divorce-an angel explains to the narrator that God is eternal, so all our life choices have already been made, but were still freely made by us. Suddenly, the narrator sees a vision of a group of figures (representing souls) manipulating pieces on a giant board (representing Earth)-and then he can't think about it anymore because his brain hurts, and the reader's does too at that point! But generally, how would the notion that an eternal being can see what I'm going to do tomorrow be compatible with that fact that I'm making decisions from moment to moment?

Also, does this mean that the course of human history is generally already decided on? I admit this question is more of interest to me than most people; I used to spend a lot of time on (and still sometimes visit) an internet community dedicated to alternate history, which meant we wrote stories about fictional countries, or real countries that had different histories and are thus vastly different places. But it was convention that one's story had to begin with a "Point of Divergence"-the event that went differently, somebody dying or living, a battle going the opposite way that it did in reality-that started history down whatever alternate road you were writing about. Likewise, it was generally believed that the things we were writing about were things that could have happened, even if they were somewhat unlikely (and we also had a lot of jokes about how unlikely reality was!). But the notion of "eternity" as stated above seems to imply that everything has already been decided. Either way, I guess, we definately created a few interesting places!

replies.title: Re: Karma and reincarnation
replies.content:
1) If I see you make a decision, does that mean that you had no free will in making that decision? The same rule applies here. An eternal being sees you freely making a decision tomorrow. The being doesn't influence your decision, you make it freely, and the eternal being sees you do so. Since the eternal being isn't in time, it isn't seeing you do something in the future, because in eternity there is no future. It's as though the being were reading an account of your actions written after the end of the world.

2) No, that's not what "eternal" means. It means outside of time. From the perspective of an eternal being, nothing has "already" been decided -- don't you see that "already" implies a position in the present, in time? There is no "already" in eternity. There is simply the whole sweep of time, seen from outside, and every being in time is free to make their own decisions; eternal beings just see how it happens to come out. (Not "in advance," either -- there is no "in advance" in eternity...)

replies.title: Re: Karma and reincarnation
replies.content:
I am not JMG, and cannot speak as a representative of his spiritual beliefs. The mysteries of karma have also been trickling through my mind as of late, though. I hope you don't mind my pontificating.

I do not believe individuals get to choose the lives they enter into - or at least, they don't have a wide variety to choose from. I see karma as a mechanism - almost machine-like - do evil, earn evil. Do good, earn a more favorable birth circumstance. But once you've wound up the mechanism through evil action or intent, it must spring... there's no escaping it.

The good side of seeing karma this way, is that it provides universal justice. No sin ever slips by unpaid for. However, the bad side is exactly what you call out - the assumption that babies with cancer somehow "earned" their suffering. (A related societal sin in the Hindu faith is assuming the oppressed lower castes somehow "earned" being born there. An argument often deployed against any societal reform...)

I have a nuanced opinion on this issue. First, every soul is equal. The monstrously mistaken soul of Hitler and the enlightened soul of Gandhi are just at different points along a similar continuum. Who is to say that the same soul who once reaped such horror on the earth, a thousand years from now, might not lead a people to salvation instead? Karma is the mechanism through which the lessons, as harsh as they are, will be comprehensively taught, and the higher potential eventually brought forth.

Second, I think that when a soul is "new," it comes into the world as a socio/psychopath. Despite being very dangerous to to the rest of us, psychologists note that socio/psychopaths are "innocent" - they don't truly understand why what they're doing is wrong. Almost all evil actions performed in this world come about 1) through the afore-mentioned "innocence," 2) self-delusion by the spiritually immature, or 3) unconquered (or unacknowledged) fears. Though the deeds performed to earn the karma were evil, the soul itself is a lot like a three-year-old stomping on ants. Or a child crying in the closet as it hides from monsters. Or a pre-teen swaggering about trying to impress their friends. "True" evil, freely chosen in a soul's adulthood, is (thankfully) rare - but the mechanism of karma shudders into motion the same for all.

Finally, we need to keep another aspect of karma in mind: the karma of everyone who interacts with the baby. Every soul is equal - this means that the former soul of Hitler is no less worthy of loving-kindness than that of Gandhi, if only for the future wonders it has the potential to perform. Souls under deep karma burdens are usually "innocent" - they are just learning in THIS life why suffering is a terrible thing to freely cause. And of course the correct, good-karma way to behave towards a sick baby is crystal clear to anyone with a developed conscience. Its parents and guardians must love and care for him or her unconditionally and seek any treatment which is available. The doctors who treat him or her must work their very hardest towards their healing. The baby's suffering is in part a reflection of their own web of karma - how they face down, or bear up under, such a comprehensive tragedy, is both in response to and triggering in turn the reverberation of their own karma mechanism.

So, in my musings, several things are true at the same time: the soul of the sick baby is certainly working through some vicious karma which it "earned" from black deeds; nevertheless, the soul is innocent, right now in the process of learning about the pains and the joys of life on this plane which will reverberate in its future incarnations; and finally, the only karma we really need to concern ourselves with is our own. So if that sick baby is part of your family or professional circle, cuddle and kiss that sick baby every chance you get, during their short time here on Earth. That is what will be remembered.

Thanks for the chance to pound this out - it was good to gather my thoughts on this topic.

comments.title: Eagles order
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Do you know anything about the Fraternal Order of the Eagles?

It seems that one of my great-grandfathers was a member of the Canadian branch and I'm interested to know if there's anything more than what is given away on their website and wiki page? (By which I mean, are they in any way an occult order, or something more akin to Masons?)

replies.title: Re: Eagles order
replies.content:
They're not an occult order. I've known some people who were active in the Eagles; it's a fraternal order that focuses on mutual help and community betterment.

What you have in the UK, by the way, are friendly societies. There aren't many of them left, and most of those have turned into insurance companies, but they used to be pretty much the same sort of organization as fraternal orders over here.

replies.title: Re: Eagles order
replies.content:
Ah, thanks for the info. They certainly sounded like a fairly nice organisation from the description (and according to the wiki page, 7 presidents, including my favourite, Theodore Roosevelt, were members), but just wondered if there was more to it.

I have heard of friendly societies, but yes, as you say, they all appear to now be pensions and insurance companies, rather than actual fraternal orders, sadly.

replies.title: Re: Eagles order
replies.content:
Some of the non-occult fraternal orders which were founded in America spread to other countries. I remember back in the Eighties a friend of mine telling me about his encounter with a busload of Welsh Moose on tour. He told me that they sang a lot, not because they were Moose, but because they were Welsh.

All of these organizations have lost membership, and perhaps can no longer support overseas chapters, but it's worth looking them up online if you have an interest in joining one. Here in northern California, the Elks seem to be doing pretty well. Some members of my family play in a bluegrass band, and they do concerts for the locals at an Elks hall regularly. The space they played in had a stage at one end, a bar on one side, and a cafeteria line on the other, so you could get a hot meal and a drink and sit at a table with friends and listen to live music. During some of the terrible wildfires we had in northern California a few years ago, another more rural Elks chapter allowed people who had been burned out of their homes to stay in their parking lot or indoors, and they fed them and were a center for services for several weeks.

comments.title: Law of Limitation
comments.content:
I've been very slowly meditating my way through the law of limitation (even before I saw your summary I just felt I needed to take time to digest it myself before seeing what you had to think in order to get as many views on the topic as possible.)

It really sticks with me because dealing with its reality seems to be a crucial blockage for me in multiple areas of my life. It's partially related to a desire for limitlessness, but it's also a bit more nuanced.

I find myself in particular having issues with the "opportunity cost" aspect of the law of limitation. Meaning: if you choose to do something, you're in essence choosing not to do a whole mess of other things.

I've operated (and still do operate) in a more unstructured mode. I forget the exact words Fortune used, but in my mind I see myself as a mostly aimless firehose of power, who pounces on moments of inspiration to direct this power at just the right moment.

I find this so much more comfortable than my view of the law of limitation, which to me involves not only sacrifice, but a crippling fear of "but what if I'm making the wrong choice."

When I first read this I even ended up using it that morning to phenomenal success. There was a larger project I wanted to embark on, I found a nice cleavage to get a bite sized chunk, and completed that chunk of work under muse-like influence.

However, that chunk remains the only part of this project completed. Whenever I attempt to pick it up again, I convince myself that I'm wasting my time, I'm going to fail, I'm deluding myself into thinking this is the right path.

In meditation I found myself thinking about how to deal with this issue and highlighted a few key issues:

- Confidence / fear of making a mistake / committing to an action. This seems more psychological in nature, albeit really difficult to get past. I wish there were an easier answer, but it seems like there are just certain truths I need to accept, and I currently can’t stop myself from doing everything in my power to avoid accepting them (seems like your will training exercises might work here… I’ve been slowly working my way through that as well.)

- How to connect what I envision on the mental plane with individual actions. Fortune mentioned that part of the skill of the law of limitation is balancing your broad and narrow view. This makes sense, but I find myself having trouble with truly linking these two views. If I’m focusing on a narrow view, I either lose or fear losing sight of the broad view. If I’m in the broad view I feel better, but find my ability to truly act towards what I want to do limited.

I'm finding it difficult to make this a bit more concise question... Does it seem like I'm on the right path in digging this out?

replies.title: Re: Law of Limitation
replies.content:
The fear of limitation is as counterproductive as the denial of limitation. As I tried to point out in my book, limitation is your friend; you can choose your limits, use them to strengthen you, and shut out things you don't want by accepting the corresponding limits. (A seat belt limits your freedom to be flung through a windshield...)

As for getting to the point that you can use this sort of thinking constructively, it takes time and practice. Relax, take it a little at a time, and remember that nobody's grading your work. ;-)

replies.content:

replies.title: Re: Law of Limitation
replies.content:
"I convince myself that I'm wasting my time, I'm going to fail, I'm deluding myself into thinking this is the right path."

If part of you believes that the outcome itself would be wrong - or you would be wrong, if you succeeded - or it would be wrong for you to succeed, even if you could - then that part of you will shut down any attempt to make and follow a step by step plan to get there.

If part of you believes the outcome would be fine, but you're doomed to incompetence and failure no matter WHAT plan you try - then that part of you will keep you from trying ANY plan.

It's not clear to me which of these beliefs you are describing.

- Mr. New-Writer

replies.title: Re: Law of Limitation
replies.content:
“In order to achieve an end you must outline that end and limit yourself to it, rejecting all that is irrelevant.” There is immense power in these words. Feel it. Memorize it. Believe it. Magic works best when it's not muddled, and the easiest way to make it clear is to do the same ritual everyday trying to achieve the same aim. The more you do it, the more synchronicities will appear in your life that will help you achieve that aim. Once you achieve your aim, move onto the next.

It's also important to keep in mind what magic can and cannot do. When I met my wife, I spent months trying to manifest "the perfect woman for me," and, through a series of "unbelievable" coincidences, I found her. That's possible. I learned mostly through years of trial and error and am still learning.

If I am reading you correctly, try to manifest small, simple things until your success in doing so dispels the belief that you're doomed to incompetence. Once that is gone, focus on creating clear win/win outcomes in your mind and focus on manifesting those. Belief comes with success. With success comes responsibility.

While I had previously had trouble with the wand, I shortened it in January, and it has worked much better since.
These experiences have not been unpleasant (in fact, it was highly enjoyable), the results were not unwanted (except when the wand started to really want to be used), and I want it to happen again, but more importantly, I want to know what I can do with this energy now that I'm able to access it.

3)I hoped to learn more about myself and work through blocks I have in my life and magical practice, so in December I attempted shadow work for the first time. Swept with my spiritual broom. Rang my little cowbell. Put my blanket down, grounded, and had only a candle and asked the cards for permission and asked them "What is wrong with me?" and "What am I afraid of?". Yes, I now know that those were bad questions, but that was when something showed up in my mind. I heard a nasty cold voice and it said that I must give up my riches (its words), and remember how, as a queer white man, I will never have it anywhere as difficult as black and brown trans women. I asked what my riches were, and the voice said that all of it was, that I don't deserve any of it, the privilege I have in having such riches as I do, not like black and brown trans women, who will never be given the opportunities I have had.
I said I had no riches to give up. The thing, the voice, was not happy with that answer, and kept asking for them, and we went round like this for a bit. Its answers got steadily less coherent the more I insisted I had no riches. I think its last response was a loud "GIVE ME!". I shut the whole work down and turned on the lights. I was hungry and tired, so I went downstairs and ate. The voice hasn't been back since, but I haven't started shadow work again. Would you recommend trying it again, or...what did I do, if anything?

replies.title: Re: Child sex abuse
replies.content:
I think it does depend very much on the culture, and also on the times.

One of my great-great-great-grandmothers, in frontier Joliet (then called Juliet), Illinois, courted her future husband at 13, married him at 14, and had her first child (my great-great-grandfather) at 15. Her husband was in his early 30s when they married, but a dashing and romantic man; and she rightly recognized that he would go far and become very wealthy. She had a long and happy married life, despite losing about bhalf of her children to various illnesses far too soon., and her reminiscences in her very old age are overflowing with contentment at her lot. And she was pleased that she beat out all the other marriageable girls of her age who had also set their caps at that same man. (In those days, any 18-year old girl who was still unmarried was already well on her way to being an unmarriageable old maid. Marriage in a girl's early or middle 'teens was not a rare exception, but quite normal.)

Of course, in such a very small frontier town, where dogs copulated freely in the streets and roosters tried to do their thing all the time every day, even at 13 she and her girlfriends would already have had a pretty good idea of what happened physically in the marriage bed, and (at a bare minimum) gt-gt-gt-grandmother figured that she could handle it just fine. She might even have been looking forward to the new experience.

comments.title: Israel Regardie and Neville Goddard
comments.content:
I just wanted to share a snippet from a transcript of Neville Goddard's lectures where I believe he mentions Israel Regardie.

Goddard was a New Thought lecturer who taught from the 1940s up to his death in 1972. Regardie was his friend when they both lived in New York and they corresponded through the WWII years as well. Regardie devoted one whole chapter to Goddard in his book The Romance of Metaphysics, but I didn't see a clear reference to Regardie in Goddard's work until I stumbled across these following quotes:

"A very close friend of mine (I haven’t seen him in years) but he was in the army until the other seventeen million were discharged. And I wrote him what I did, but he was a Freudian, and he knew exactly how this thing should not work, so he never applied it. So he remained for the next four years in the army. And to this day he will still not believe it. But he told me himself, “When I come to your meetings, I like it, but I put my feet into the carpet and I stick my hands on the chair and I hold on firmly to keep my sense of the profoundness and the reality of things.” So for the next four years he was still holding onto the chair in his barracks, and he wanted out as much as I did. I told him exactly how to do it, to believe in the Lord, actually believe in the Lord, and the Lord is my own wonderful I-am-ness."

"It was the year 1943 and the month was March. My friend was my age and he could have gotten out that very month, but he would not believe me. He did not believe in God. He thought he believed in God. And he wrote a book, and in this book he wrote a chapter on me, and he said, “Neville is an atheist.” Well, an atheist is one who does not believe in God. An agnostic is one who is on the verge, he doesn’t know whether he believes in God or not. He’s not against God, but he’s not yet convinced. But an atheist is a Khrushchev, one who does not believe in God.

I do not know of anyone who walks the face of this earth who believes more in God than I do. Because I stood in the presence of God and God is man: It is yourself personified."

Context: Goddard talks about how night after night, he assumed that he was sleeping in his own bed in his own home, not on a furlough, but completely discharged from the army. His superior officer decided after 9 days of first dismissing his application to discharge him. Mitch Horowitz found corresponding army records that showed Goddard was indeed discharged about 4 months into his service in the middle of WWII. Regardie does indeed quote Goddard as being an "atheist" in the book.

Anyway, I thought it might interest you. I do find it surprising that Regardie was interested in Goddard's ideas but hesitated on doing a similar exercise for himself after all his years of training in magic though.

Regardie was a complex, conflicted person, and I think it's fair to say that he didn't believe in God -- his irritable comments about invocations that resemble prayers show that. Keep in mind, though, that he may have stayed in the army because he thought it was worth fighting the Nazis...
